turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Thomas has the opinion of the Court this morning in case 16-712, Oil States Energy Services v. Greene's Energy Group.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: This case comes to us on a Writ of Certiorari to the United States Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit. The America Invents Act establishes a process called Inter Partes Review. Under that process the Patent and Trademark Office can, in limited circumstances, reconsider and cancel an issued patent claim. In this case respondent Greene's Energy Group challenged two claims in a patent owned by petitioner Oil States Energy Services. The Patent Trial and Appeal Board, the body that conducts Inter Partes Review, decided that the challenged claims were unpatentable. Oil States appealed, arguing that Inter Partes Review violates Article 3 of the Constitution and the Seventh Amendment. The Federal Circuit rejected both arguments. We affirm. We hold that Inter Partes Review does not violate Article 3. The Constitution vests the judicial power in Article 3 Courts and Congress cannot confer that power on non-Article 3 entities. When determining what can and cannot be adjudicated outside of Court, our precedents have distinguished between private rights and public rights. Generally speaking, private rights must go to Court, but public rights can be adjudicated outside of court. We conclude that Inter Partes Review falls squarely on the public rights side of the line and can be adjudicated by the Patent Office. A patent is a public franchise granted by the government that gives the owner a limited monopoly over his invention. For that reason this Court held over a century ago that the initial decision to grant patent falls within the Public Rights Doctrine. Inter Partes Review is simply a reconsideration of the decision to grant a patent, so it too falls within the Public Rights Doctrine. When the Patent Office issues a patent, it reserves the right to cancel it in Inter Partes Review and this Court has long held that public franchises can be qualified in this manner. Oil States contends that when this country was founded challenges to the validity of a patent were adjudicated exclusively in court. It is true that issues of patent validity were generally decided by English courts of law in the 18th Century, but there was another means of canceling a patent that closely resembles Inter Partes Review, petitions to the Privy Council to vacate a patent. The Privy Council was a prominent feature of the English patent system and was well-known to the framers. Nothing in the text or history of the Constitution suggests that the framers took this option away from Congress. Because Inter Partes Review does not violate Article 3, we also hold that it does not violate the Seventh Amendment. For these reasons and others set forth in our opinion, we affirm the judgment of the Federal Circuit. Justice Breyer has filed a concurring opinion, in which Justices Ginsburg and Sotomayor have joined. Justice Gorsuch has filed a dissenting opinion, in which the Chief Justice has joined.
